front: What is a model in economics?
back: A model is a simplified representation of reality.

front: What does the Production Possibility Curve (PPC) represent?
back: All possible combinations of two goods that can be produced with the available resources if they are used efficiently.

front: What is an efficient production point?
back: A point on the PPC where resources are fully used; producing more of one good requires producing less of the other.

front: What is an inefficient production point?
back: A point inside the PPC where resources are underutilized; output of at least one good can increase without reducing the other.

front: What is an unattainable production point?
back: A point outside the PPC that cannot be produced with current resources and technology.
An agent has absolute advantage in an activity if they can perform it using fewer resources than another agent.
The value of the next best alternative that must be given up to take a particular action.

front: How is opportunity cost linked to the slope of the PPC?
back: Opportunity cost equals the slope of the PPC: it measures how much of one good must be forgone to produce more of the other.
An agent has comparative advantage in producing a good when their opportunity cost of producing that good is lower than another agent's.

front: What is the principle of comparative advantage?
back: Everyone can be better off when each agent or country specializes in the activities for which it has comparative advantage and then trades.

front: What is the principle of increasing opportunity cost?
back: As production of a good expands, producers with the lowest opportunity cost are used first; later units come from higher-cost producers, so opportunity cost rises.

front: Why does the economy-wide PPC bow outward from the origin?
back: Because resources differ in opportunity cost. The economy uses low-cost producers first, so producing additional units becomes increasingly costly.

front: What is the Consumption Possibility Curve (CPC)?
back: All possible combinations of two goods that the agents in an economy can consume.

front: What is the difference between PPC and CPC?
back: PPC describes what an economy can produce. CPC describes what it can consume. With trade, CPC can lie beyond the PPC.
Because trade expands consumption opportunities. A country's welfare depends on what it can consume, not just what it can produce.
